justifications.clarity: The roadmap subsection (§1.3) maps every subsequent section explicitly, and five formal definitions with precise variable mappings ensure progressive, unambiguous exposition; one deduction for the grammar slip 'hallucinate' (should be 'hallucinates') in the hybrid-methods paragraph of §6.4.
justifications.rigor: The FCFF-FCFE proposition is proved from Modigliani-Miller first principles; g < WACC is enforced both in the Monte Carlo definition and in numpy clipping; first-order error-propagation partial derivatives are numerically verified in-text; one deduction for the proof's telegraphic 'rearranging' step and for the unverified ~$15.2B DCF example in §2.1 that appears inconsistent with the $13.3B ReAct trace in §4 (different assumed inputs).
justifications.completeness: All 8 learning objectives are addressed across 9 sections with 11 Python listings, 18+ labelled equations, and an end-to-end case study; however, WACC — the most sensitive DCF input — is used throughout but never derived or estimated (no CAPM or WACC-estimation subsection), and the benchmarking subsection presents only qualitative 'preliminary evidence' without a concrete empirical results table.
justifications.pedagogy: Learning objectives map one-to-one to sections and difficulty progresses from single-firm FCFF computation through multi-agent pipeline orchestration; the annotated ReAct trace is exceptionally pedagogical; one deduction for providing only a single exercise at the end of §5, leaving readers without structured practice on FCFF computation, forecast evaluation, or comparable selection.
justifications.style: Prose is natural and avoids filler; FCFF/DA/WACC notation is consistent; LaTeX environments and labels follow conventions with no \paragraph{} headings; minor deductions for the 'hallucinate' grammatical error, Python 3.10+ union-type hints without a version note, and the figure caption's vague 'most recently reported free cash flow' without a date anchor.
